slides.title: Welcome: Prompt Engineering for Marketers
bullets: By the end of today you can write prompts that save you time on real marketing tasks
No coding, no technical background needed — just clear thinking and good writing
We will move from concept to practice within the first hour

speaker_notes.aim: Set a welcoming, low-threshold tone and immediately signal relevance for marketers
speaker_notes.instructions: Open with a warm welcome. Show the slide and read the bullets aloud, emphasizing 'real marketing tasks' to anchor the day in practical relevance. Avoid starting with definitions — keep it human.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Before we dive in: who here has already tried ChatGPT or any AI tool for work, even once? Raise your hand.
speaker_notes.debrief: Acknowledge the spread of experience in the room. Reassure beginners that everyone will leave with usable skills. Note that even experienced users will learn a structured approach today.

slides.title: What You Will Be Able to Do After Today
bullets: Write a prompt that produces a first draft of a social media post in your brand voice
Use AI to generate five campaign headline variations in under two minutes
Give feedback to an AI output and refine it through follow-up prompts
Avoid the three most common prompting mistakes that produce generic, unusable content
Build a personal prompt template you can reuse every week

speaker_notes.aim: Make learning goals concrete and motivating by framing them as marketing wins, not abstract skills
speaker_notes.instructions: Walk through each bullet slowly. Ask participants to mentally tick which goals feel most relevant to their own role. Do not rush — this is where buy-in is created.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Which of these five outcomes would save you the most time right now in your daily work?
speaker_notes.debrief: Let two or three people answer. Use their answers to signal that the training is designed around exactly those needs. Write down any specific tasks mentioned — you can reference them later in the exercises.

slides.title: Energizer: The Instruction Game
bullets: You have 90 seconds: write instructions for a colleague to make a perfect cup of coffee
Your colleague must follow ONLY what you wrote — nothing more, nothing less
We will read two examples aloud and see what happens

speaker_notes.aim: Create a memorable, fun experience that directly mirrors the challenge of writing good prompts — vague instructions produce vague results
speaker_notes.instructions: Hand out sticky notes or ask participants to type in the chat if remote. Set a visible 90-second timer. After writing, ask two volunteers to read their instructions. Playfully act out following them literally — for example, if they say 'add milk' ask 'how much? warm or cold? before or after the coffee?'
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What made some instructions work and others fail?
speaker_notes.debrief: Bridge to AI: 'This is exactly what happens when you give an AI a vague prompt. The AI follows your instructions literally and fills gaps with assumptions. Today we learn how to close those gaps intentionally.'

slides.title: Agenda: Our Three Hours Together
bullets: 09:00 – 09:20 | Kick-off: goals, energizer, agenda
09:20 – 10:20 | Theory: how prompting works and the CRAFT framework
10:35 – 11:05 | Example: live prompting for a real campaign brief
11:05 – 11:50 | Exercise: write and refine your own marketing prompts
11:50 – 12:00 | Wrap-up: takeaways, your prompt template, next steps

speaker_notes.aim: Give participants a clear map of the day so they can mentally pace themselves
speaker_notes.instructions: Show the agenda briefly. Point out that the majority of the time is hands-on. Mention that the break is fixed but questions are welcome throughout.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Any questions about the structure before we start?
speaker_notes.debrief: Quickly address any logistical questions, then move forward. Keep this slide short — it is orientation, not content.

slides.title: What Is a Prompt — And Why Does It Matter?
bullets: A prompt is the instruction you give to an AI tool like ChatGPT, Gemini, or Copilot
The AI has no memory, no context, and no goals — it only responds to what you write
A weak prompt gives you a generic response you cannot use; a strong prompt gives you a draft you can edit
Think of the AI as a very fast, very literal junior copywriter who needs exact briefing
Your prompting skill determines the quality of the output — not the AI's intelligence

speaker_notes.aim: Establish the foundational mental model: prompt quality = output quality, and the marketer is in control
speaker_notes.instructions: Explain the 'junior copywriter' analogy in your own words. Ask if anyone has been frustrated by AI giving them something generic or off-brand. Use that frustration as motivation for what comes next.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What is the worst AI output you have ever received? What do you think caused it?
speaker_notes.debrief: Validate frustration and reframe it: 'That was not the AI's fault or yours — you just did not have a system yet. That system is what we are building today.'

slides.title: How AI Language Models Actually Work (Simply Put)
bullets: AI models are trained on vast amounts of text and learn patterns in language
They predict the most likely next word based on your input — they do not 'think' or 'understand'
This means the AI will always produce something plausible-sounding, even if it is wrong or generic
The more context and specificity you give, the more the AI can narrow its prediction to what you actually need
You are not searching for information — you are steering a prediction engine

speaker_notes.aim: Give participants just enough technical understanding to make smart prompting decisions, without overwhelming them
speaker_notes.instructions: Use the analogy of autocomplete on a phone: 'Imagine autocomplete on steroids, trained on the entire internet.' Emphasize that this is why vague prompts produce vague outputs — the AI finds the average answer when given little direction.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Knowing the AI is essentially predicting text, what does that change about how you would write your next prompt?
speaker_notes.debrief: Summarize: 'The more specific your context, the less the AI has to guess, and the more useful the output becomes. This is the entire logic behind what we are about to learn.'

slides.title: The CRAFT Framework: Your Prompting Formula
bullets: C — Context: who are you, what is the situation, what is the brand?
R — Role: tell the AI what role to play (e.g. 'Act as a senior B2B copywriter')
A — Action: state exactly what you want the AI to produce
F — Format: specify length, structure, tone, bullet points or paragraphs
T — Target: describe your audience — who will read this content?

speaker_notes.aim: Introduce the core framework participants will use throughout the training and take home
speaker_notes.instructions: Write the acronym CRAFT on a whiteboard or flipchart so it stays visible all day. Go through each letter with a one-sentence marketing example. Tell participants this framework works for 90% of their daily tasks. Do not rush this slide — it is the backbone of the whole training.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Looking at these five elements, which one do you think you currently leave out the most when using AI?
speaker_notes.debrief: Common answer: Context and Target. Validate this — most beginners write only the Action. Explain that the other four elements are what separate a usable result from a generic one.

slides.title: Breaking Down CRAFT: What Each Element Looks Like
bullets: Context: 'We are a Dutch sustainable fashion brand targeting urban women aged 25–40. Our tone is bold, warm, and never corporate.'
Role: 'Act as an experienced Instagram copywriter who specializes in lifestyle brands.'
Action: 'Write three Instagram captions for a new summer collection launch.'
Format: 'Each caption should be max 150 characters, include one emoji, and end with a call to action.'
Target: 'Our audience values sustainability and aesthetics equally — they follow brands like Patagonia and COS.'

speaker_notes.aim: Make CRAFT tangible by showing what each element looks like in real marketing language
speaker_notes.instructions: Show each bullet as a completed sentence. Point out how together they form a full, rich prompt. Ask participants to notice how much information the AI now has to work with versus a prompt like 'write me an Instagram caption for summer fashion.'
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What would happen to the output if we removed the Target element from this prompt?
speaker_notes.debrief: Answer: the AI would write for a generic audience and the result would feel bland. Reinforce that every CRAFT element removes a decision the AI would otherwise make poorly on your behalf.

slides.title: The Three Most Common Prompting Mistakes Marketers Make
bullets: Mistake 1 — Too vague: 'Write a newsletter' gives the AI nothing to work with; always specify topic, audience, length, and goal
Mistake 2 — No brand voice: without tone guidance the AI defaults to generic corporate language that sounds like no one
Mistake 3 — Accepting the first output: AI first drafts are starting points, not finished products — always refine with follow-up prompts
Bonus mistake: Asking for too many things at once — one clear task per prompt produces better results than five combined

speaker_notes.aim: Prevent the most common failure patterns before participants hit them in the exercises
speaker_notes.instructions: For each mistake, give a real example of a bad prompt and what it produces. If you have access to a live AI tool, demonstrate Mistake 1 and Mistake 2 in real time — type 'write a newsletter' and show the generic result, then add CRAFT and show the difference.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Which of these mistakes do you recognize from your own experience with AI tools?
speaker_notes.debrief: Normalize the mistakes — everyone makes them at the start. Reframe: 'Now that you can name these mistakes, you will catch yourself before making them. That is already a skill.'

slides.title: Iteration: The Skill That Multiplies Your Results
bullets: Prompting is a conversation, not a one-shot command — expect to send 2 to 4 follow-up messages
If the tone is off: 'Make this warmer and less formal — it currently sounds like a press release'
If the length is wrong: 'Shorten this to three sentences without losing the main benefit'
If the angle is missing: 'Rewrite this focusing on the sustainability angle, not the design'
Save prompts that work well — build a personal library of your best prompts over time

speaker_notes.aim: Shift the mindset from 'one perfect prompt' to 'a productive conversation with the AI'
speaker_notes.instructions: Emphasize that the best prompt engineers iterate fast, not perfectly. Show the three follow-up example sentences and explain that these are reusable phrases — participants should note them down. Mention that saving good prompts is like building a marketing asset.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What is one piece of marketing content you currently spend too much time on that iteration prompting could speed up?
speaker_notes.debrief: Use participant answers to preview the exercise. 'We will practice exactly that kind of iteration in thirty minutes. Hold that thought.'

slides.title: A Note on AI Ethics and Brand Safety
bullets: Never enter sensitive client data, personal data, or confidential campaign budgets into public AI tools
Always fact-check AI outputs — AI confidently generates plausible but incorrect statistics and dates
AI-generated content must be reviewed and edited by a human before publication — you are responsible
Check your company's policy on approved AI tools before using new platforms
Disclose AI involvement in content creation where required by platform guidelines or client agreements

speaker_notes.aim: Establish responsible use habits before participants start experimenting, without creating fear or paralysis
speaker_notes.instructions: Keep this section brief and practical, not legalistic. Frame it as professional hygiene, not a warning. If your organization has a specific AI policy, reference it here. Emphasize the human review step — AI is a collaborator, not a replacement for judgment.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Does your team currently have a shared agreement about how to use AI tools? If not, what would be the one rule you would want?
speaker_notes.debrief: Note any useful answers. Suggest that creating a short team agreement is a concrete next step they can take after the training.

slides.title: Live Example: Campaign Brief for a Product Launch
bullets: Situation: a Dutch health food brand is launching a new oat-based protein bar targeting busy young professionals
The brief asks for: 3 LinkedIn post options, 1 email subject line variant list (5 options), and a short product description for the website
We will build one CRAFT prompt that handles the LinkedIn posts — step by step, live
Then we will show how two follow-up prompts transform the first output into something usable

speaker_notes.aim: Ground the theory in a realistic, recognizable marketing scenario that participants can immediately relate to
speaker_notes.instructions: Introduce the scenario clearly before touching the AI tool. Give participants a printed or on-screen version of the brief so they can follow along. Ask: 'If this brief landed on your desk today, where would you start?' Then show how CRAFT structures the answer.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What information from this brief would you put into each letter of CRAFT?
speaker_notes.debrief: Take 2–3 answers. Then say: 'Let me show you how I would build this prompt. Watch what happens at each step.'

slides.title: Building the CRAFT Prompt — Live
bullets: C: 'We are a Dutch health food brand called VitalOat. Our tone is energetic, science-backed, and friendly — never preachy.'
R: 'Act as a LinkedIn content strategist with experience in the food and wellness industry.'
A: 'Write 3 LinkedIn posts announcing the launch of our new oat protein bar.'
F: 'Each post should be 80–120 words, start with a hook, include one specific product benefit, and end with a question to drive engagement.'
T: 'Our audience is professionals aged 25–35 in the Netherlands who care about nutrition but have little time to cook.'

speaker_notes.aim: Demonstrate the CRAFT framework applied to a real prompt, showing participants exactly what a well-constructed prompt looks like
speaker_notes.instructions: Type this prompt live into the AI tool with the audience watching. Build it element by element — do not paste it all at once. Narrate each addition: 'Now I am adding the Target because I want the AI to know who will read this.' After submitting, read the output aloud and ask participants to evaluate it.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Looking at the output: what worked well? What would you still change?
speaker_notes.debrief: Collect 2–3 observations. Use them to set up the next slide on iteration — 'Great, now let us fix those things with a follow-up prompt.'

slides.title: Iteration in Action: Two Follow-Up Prompts
bullets: Follow-up 1 — Adjusting tone: 'The second post is too formal. Rewrite it with a more conversational, almost casual tone — as if a colleague is recommending it to a friend.'
Follow-up 2 — Sharpening the hook: 'The opening lines of all three posts are weak. Rewrite only the first sentence of each to be more provocative or surprising — something that stops a scroller.'
Result: in under 5 minutes we went from a brief to three near-publishable LinkedIn posts
Total prompt count: 3. Total time: approximately 4 minutes of writing, 1 minute of AI generation

speaker_notes.aim: Show that iteration is fast, targeted, and produces dramatically better results than accepting the first output
speaker_notes.instructions: Type the follow-up prompts live. Make a point of not rewriting the entire prompt — show that you are only addressing the specific problem. After each follow-up, compare the new output to the previous one and name the improvement explicitly.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What follow-up prompt would you write if the output was great in tone but the product benefit was too vague?
speaker_notes.debrief: Let one participant suggest a follow-up. Try it live if time allows. This moment of co-creation is highly memorable and builds confidence.

slides.title: What We Just Did — Mapping It Back to CRAFT
bullets: We started with a full CRAFT prompt — Context, Role, Action, Format, Target all present
The first output was usable but not great — this is normal and expected
Follow-up prompt 1 targeted the tone (Format + Context refinement)
Follow-up prompt 2 targeted a specific structural element (Action refinement)
The final output required only minor human editing before it could be scheduled

speaker_notes.aim: Help participants connect the live demonstration back to the framework so they can replicate the process independently
speaker_notes.instructions: Use this slide as a debrief of the example. Map each step explicitly to CRAFT. Reinforce that the process is repeatable — participants should feel they could do this themselves right now. Transition directly into the exercise with energy.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Do you feel ready to try this yourself with a real task from your own work?
speaker_notes.debrief: Use enthusiasm from the room to launch the exercise. If there is hesitation, normalize it: 'Your first prompt does not need to be perfect — it just needs to exist. We will improve it together.'

slides.title: Exercise Overview: Your Own Marketing Prompt
bullets: Duration: 35 minutes total — 20 minutes individual work, 15 minutes pair review and group share
Step 1: Choose one real marketing task from your current work (social post, email subject line, ad copy, blog intro, product description)
Step 2: Build a complete CRAFT prompt for that task using the template on the next slide
Step 3: Run the prompt, evaluate the output, and write at least one follow-up prompt
Step 4: Share your best output and one lesson learned with a partner

speaker_notes.aim: Brief participants clearly so they can work independently without needing repeated clarification
speaker_notes.instructions: Read the steps aloud and check for understanding. Emphasize 'real marketing task' — not a made-up scenario. If someone cannot think of one, have a list of backup tasks ready: write three Instagram captions for an upcoming event, generate five email subject line options for a monthly newsletter, write a short LinkedIn post announcing a company milestone.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What task have you already decided you want to work on? Say it out loud to your neighbor.
speaker_notes.debrief: The act of saying the task aloud commits participants to it. Circulate during the exercise to support anyone who is stuck on the CRAFT structure.

slides.title: Your CRAFT Prompt Template — Fill This In
bullets: C — Context: [Describe your brand, product, or campaign situation in 2–3 sentences]
R — Role: [Tell the AI what expert role to play — e.g. 'Act as a senior email marketing specialist']
A — Action: [State exactly what content you want — format, number of variations, specific ask]
F — Format: [Specify word count or character limit, tone, structure, any mandatory elements]
T — Target: [Describe the specific audience who will read this — demographics, values, platform]

speaker_notes.aim: Give participants a structured scaffold they can fill in immediately without having to remember the framework from memory
speaker_notes.time: 1 minute to introduce, then participants work independently
speaker_notes.instructions: Hand out a printed version of this template or share it in the digital workspace. Tell participants they can copy-paste their completed template directly into the AI tool as their first prompt. Remind them the template is also something they take home and reuse.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Which element of CRAFT is hardest for you to fill in right now?
speaker_notes.debrief: Address the hardest element during your circulation. Common answer: Context — help participants describe their brand voice if they are unsure how to articulate it.

slides.title: During the Exercise: Coaching Prompts to Guide You
bullets: If the output sounds too generic: add more specific brand voice cues to your Context — e.g. 'We never use exclamation marks and always use 'you' directly'
If the output is too long: add to Format — 'Shorten to maximum 3 sentences and cut any filler phrases'
If the angle is wrong: add to Action — 'Rewrite focusing only on the benefit of saving time, not on the product features'
If you want more variety: 'Give me three completely different creative angles for the same message'
If you love one part: 'Keep the first paragraph exactly as it is and rewrite only the closing call to action'

speaker_notes.aim: Give participants a cheat sheet of targeted follow-up prompts so they do not get stuck when the first output is not quite right
speaker_notes.time: Ongoing reference during the 20-minute individual work phase
speaker_notes.instructions: Keep this slide visible on screen throughout the individual work phase. Tell participants these are rescue prompts — if they get stuck, they can use any of these. Circulate actively and refer participants to specific bullets when you see them struggling.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: After your first output: which of these coaching prompts do you need right now?
speaker_notes.debrief: After 20 minutes, call time on individual work. Ask: 'Who has an output they are proud of? Who has an output that surprised them?'

slides.title: Pair Review and Group Share
bullets: Share your final prompt and best output with your partner — give each other 2 minutes to read
Feedback question 1: 'What do you think was the strongest element of their CRAFT prompt?'
Feedback question 2: 'What one change would make the output even more on-brand or usable?'
Group share: two pairs will show their prompt and output to the full group and explain one thing they learned
We are looking for: what worked, what surprised you, and what you will do differently next time

speaker_notes.aim: Consolidate learning through structured peer feedback and make individual insights collective
speaker_notes.time: 15 minutes — 5 minutes pair review, 10 minutes group share
speaker_notes.instructions: Pair participants who worked on different types of tasks if possible — this increases the diversity of examples. For group share, select pairs who had interestingly different experiences (one who got a great first output, one who needed several iterations). Facilitate the debrief by drawing out the patterns you observed while circulating.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What is one thing your partner did in their prompt that you are going to steal for your own work?
speaker_notes.debrief: Celebrate concrete wins. Name specific things that worked well and explain why they worked in terms of CRAFT. This reinforces the framework through recognition.

slides.title: What You Can Now Do: Your Five New Skills
bullets: Skill 1: Use the CRAFT framework to build complete, structured prompts for any marketing task
Skill 2: Give the AI a specific role and brand context so outputs sound like your brand, not a generic template
Skill 3: Identify weak outputs and know exactly which element of the prompt to fix
Skill 4: Iterate with targeted follow-up prompts instead of rewriting from scratch
Skill 5: Save and reuse your best prompts to build a personal marketing prompt library

speaker_notes.aim: Close the learning loop by naming exactly what participants can do now — making the learning tangible and confidence-building
speaker_notes.instructions: Read each skill and briefly link it back to something that happened in the training — the energizer, the live demo, their own exercise. This narrative callback makes the learning feel earned, not just taught.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: Which of these five skills feels most new to you compared to where you started this morning?
speaker_notes.debrief: Let two or three people answer. Acknowledge the growth explicitly. Transition to the next slide on practical application.

slides.title: From Training to Daily Practice: Your Action Plan
bullets: This week: use CRAFT for one real task per day — choose low-stakes content like social posts or email drafts to build confidence
This week: save every prompt that produces a good output in a shared doc or Notion page titled 'Our Prompt Library'
Next two weeks: identify the three content types your team produces most often and build a reusable CRAFT template for each
This month: share one prompt win in your team meeting — what you prompted, what you got, how much time it saved
Ongoing: when a prompt fails, diagnose which CRAFT element was missing before giving up on the tool

speaker_notes.aim: Make transfer to real work concrete by giving participants specific, time-bound actions rather than vague intentions
speaker_notes.instructions: Ask participants to write down or highlight the one action they will definitely do this week. If time allows, have them share it with the person next to them as a commitment. The shared prompt library idea often sparks team enthusiasm — encourage it.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What is the very first piece of content you will write using CRAFT when you get back to your desk?
speaker_notes.debrief: Let three people share their answers. The specificity of real planned actions is a strong signal that transfer will happen. Make note of common answers to include in your follow-up communication.

slides.title: Your Take-Home Toolkit
bullets: CRAFT prompt template (printed and digital) — use it for every new content type
Rescue prompt cheat sheet — the five follow-up prompts for fixing common output problems
Recommended tools to start with: ChatGPT (chatgpt.com), Microsoft Copilot (if your organization uses Microsoft 365), Google Gemini
One recommended next step: spend 15 minutes this week prompting something you would normally spend 2 hours writing — compare the results
Questions after today? Reach out to [trainer contact] or post in [team Slack/Teams channel]

speaker_notes.aim: Send participants away with clear resources, a low-bar first action, and a support channel so momentum is not lost
speaker_notes.instructions: Distribute the printed toolkit if available. Walk through each item briefly. Emphasize the 15-minute challenge as a specific, achievable first step. End the training on an energetic, forward-looking note — not a summary, but a launch.
speaker_notes.reflective_question: What is one word to describe how you feel about using AI for your marketing work now compared to when you walked in this morning?
speaker_notes.debrief: Do a quick word-around the room — one word each. Common shift: from 'unsure' or 'confused' to 'curious' or 'ready.' Celebrate that shift explicitly. Close with: 'The best prompt you will ever write is the next one you try.'
